title: Clone graph
description: Clone a graph given adjacency list

examples.explanation: Input: Node1(neighbors:2,4), Node2(1,3), Node3(2,4), Node4(1,3). Step 1: cloneGraph(Node1). visited={}. dfs(Node1): clone1=Node(1). visited={Node1}. neighbors: Node2 not visited, dfs(Node2): clone2=Node(2). visited={Node1,Node2}. neighbors: Node1 visited, find clone: scan visited, match Node1 -> clone1, add to clone2.neighbors. Node3 not visited, dfs(Node3): clone3=Node(3). visited={Node1,Node2,Node3}. neighbors: Node2 visited, scan visited for Node2 -> clone2, add. Node4 not visited, dfs(Node4): clone4=Node(4). visited={Node1,Node2,Node3,Node4}. neighbors: Node1 visited, scan for Node1 -> clone1, add. Node3 visited, scan for Node3 -> clone3, add. Return clone4. Back to dfs(Node3): add clone4 to clone3.neighbors. Return clone3. Back to dfs(Node2): add clone3 to clone2.neighbors. Return clone2. Back to dfs(Node1): neighbors: Node2 visited, scan for Node2 -> clone2, add. Node4 visited, scan for Node4 -> clone4, add. Return clone1. Output: clone1 connected to clone2,clone4; clone2 to clone1,clone3; clone3 to clone2,clone4; clone4 to clone1,clone3.

examples.output: Cloned single node with no neighbors
examples.explanation: Input: Node1(neighbors:[]). Step 1: cloneGraph(Node1). visited={}. dfs(Node1): clone1=Node(1). visited={Node1}. neighbors: empty list, no processing. Return clone1. Output: Node(1) with neighbors=[].

approaches.name: Brute Force DFS with Set O(n²)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS to traverse the graph, using a set to track visited nodes and avoid cycles, but clone each node by iterating through all nodes to find neighbors each time, leading to quadratic time. This is inefficient and not practical for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of graph cloning using DFS with visited set and inefficient neighbor lookup.
ladders.explanation: This solution clones an undirected graph represented by adjacency lists using a brute force approach. The intuition is to traverse the original graph depth-first while avoiding cycles with a visited set, but instead of efficiently mapping original nodes to clones, we search for neighbors by scanning all nodes each time. We start with the given node, create its clone, mark it visited, then recursively process neighbors. For each neighbor, we check if it's visited; if not, we recursively clone it. To add edges between clones, we need to find the clone corresponding to each neighbor. Since we don't maintain a direct mapping, we search through all visited nodes by comparing values (assuming unique node values) or references, which requires O(n) per neighbor lookup. This leads to O(n²) total time where n is the number of nodes. The space complexity is O(n) for the visited set and recursion stack. The algorithm works because DFS ensures we visit all nodes exactly once, and the brute force neighbor finding, while inefficient, correctly identifies clones. Step-by-step: 1. Define a Node class with value and neighbors list. 2. Define cloneGraph function taking original node. 3. If node is null, return null. 4. Initialize visited set. 5. Define recursive dfs function: a. Create new node with same value. b. Add to visited. c. For each neighbor in original node's neighbors: i. If neighbor not visited, recursively call dfs. ii. Find clone of neighbor by scanning visited nodes (O(n) search). iii. Add found clone to current clone's neighbors. d. Return clone. 6. Call dfs on input and return result. Connection to L1: This uses the DFS traversal with visited tracking from L1 but adds the inefficient neighbor resolution. Common mistakes include not handling null input, infinite recursion from cycles without visited set, incorrect neighbor linking due to search errors, or assuming nodes have unique values. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val=0, neighbors=None): self.val = val; self.neighbors = neighbors if neighbors is not None else []. def cloneGraph(node): if not node: return None; visited = set(); def dfs(orig): clone = Node(orig.val); visited.add(orig); for nb in orig.neighbors: if nb not in visited: dfs(nb); for v in visited: if v == nb: clone.neighbors.append(Node(v.val)); break; return clone; return dfs(node). Time: O(n²), Space: O(n).

examples.trace: DFS traversal with visited set. For each neighbor, inefficiently scan visited nodes to find corresponding clone and link it. Final cloned graph matches original structure.

examples.output: Cloned single node with no neighbors
examples.trace: Input: Node1(neighbors:[]). Step 1: cloneGraph(Node1). visited={}. dfs(Node1): clone1=Node(1). visited={Node1}. neighbors: empty list, no processing. Return clone1. Output: Node(1) with neighbors=[].

testCases.expected: Node1 connected to Node2, Node2 to Node1
Invalid input due to self-loop in undirected graph

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS/BFS Cloning
ladders.desc: Depth-first search through graph nodes while tracking visited nodes to avoid cycles.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements DFS traversal on an undirected graph using a visited set to prevent infinite recursion in cyclic graphs. The intuition is to explore as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, which systematically visits all nodes. The visited set ensures each node is processed exactly once, critical for graphs with cycles. Step-by-step: 1. Define a Node class. 2. Define dfs function taking current node and visited set. 3. If node is null, return. 4. Mark node as visited. 5. Process node (e.g., print value). 6. For each neighbor in node.neighbors: if neighbor not visited, recursively call dfs. Time complexity is O(n + e) where n is nodes, e is edges, but since we only track nodes, O(n) for visited operations. Space complexity is O(n) for visited set and recursion stack. Connection to L0: This traversal forms the backbone of the clone algorithm; L0 adds cloning and neighbor linking. Common mistakes include forgetting to check for null, not initializing visited set, or modifying visited during iteration. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, visited): if not node: return; visited.add(node); print(node.val); for nb in node.neighbors: if nb not in visited: dfs(nb, visited). This standalone sub-problem can be coded to traverse and print nodes.

examples.trace: Start dfs(Node1, visited={}). visited={Node1}, print 1. neighbors: Node2 not visited, dfs(Node2, visited={Node1}): visited={Node1,Node2}, print 2. neighbors: Node1 visited, skip. Return. Back to Node1: Node3 not visited, dfs(Node3, visited={Node1,Node2}): visited={Node1,Node2,Node3}, print 3. neighbors: Node1 visited, skip. Return. Done.

dfs(Node1, {}): visited={Node1}, print 1. neighbors: Node2 not visited, dfs(Node2, {Node1}): visited={Node1,Node2}, print 2. neighbors: Node1 visited, skip; Node3 not visited, dfs(Node3, {Node1,Node2}): visited={Node1,Node2,Node3}, print 3. neighbors: Node2 visited, skip. Return. Done.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Memoization / Mapping
ladders.desc: Inefficient O(n) search through visited nodes to find corresponding clone for each neighbor.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves finding a clone node corresponding to an original neighbor by scanning all visited nodes. In the clone graph problem, after cloning a node, we need to add edges to its clone neighbors. Without a direct mapping (like hash map), we must search through the set of visited original nodes to match the neighbor, then use its clone. The intuition is that since we track visited original nodes during DFS, we can iterate through them to find a match by comparing node references or values. This is brute force because it takes O(n) time per neighbor lookup, leading to O(n²) overall. Step-by-step: 1. Maintain a visited set of original nodes. 2. When processing a neighbor for a cloned node: a. Check if neighbor's original is in visited. b. If yes, iterate through visited set. c. For each visited original node, compare if it equals the neighbor (using reference or value). d. Once found, access its corresponding clone (assuming clones are stored parallelly). e. Add clone to current clone's neighbors. Time: O(n) per lookup, Space: O(1) extra. Connection to L1: This logic is added on top of DFS traversal to handle neighbor linking. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison (e.g., by value when values not unique), not handling case when neighbor not visited yet, or inefficient scanning. Real code pattern: clone_neighbors = []; for nb in original.neighbors: found_clone = None; for v in visited: if v == nb: found_clone = clone_of_v; break; clone_neighbors.append(found_clone). This can be a standalone function to practice linear search in a set.

examples.input: visited = {Node1, Node2, Node3}, neighbor = Node2, clones = {Node1:Clone1, Node2:Clone2, Node3:Clone3}
examples.trace: Step 1: neighbor is Node2. Step 2: iterate visited: Node1 != Node2, Node2 == Node2 match. Step 3: get clone_of_v for Node2 which is Clone2. Step 4: return Clone2.
Step 1: neighbor Node1. Step 2: iterate visited: Node1 == Node1 match. Step 3: clone is Clone1. Return Clone1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Understanding how graphs are represented using nodes and neighbor lists.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers the representation of an undirected graph using nodes with adjacency lists, which is essential for the clone problem. Each node has a value and a list of references to its neighboring nodes. The intuition is that this representation allows efficient traversal and modification. For example, Node1 with value 1 and neighbors [Node2, Node3] means edges between Node1-Node2 and Node1-Node3. In the clone problem, we need to replicate this structure. Step-by-step: 1. Define a Node class with attributes: val (integer) and neighbors (list of Node objects). 2. Initialize neighbors as empty list if not provided. 3. To build a graph, create Node instances and set their neighbors lists. 4. Traversal involves accessing node.neighbors to get adjacent nodes. Time and space depend on operations; representation itself uses O(n + e) space. Connection to L2: This representation is what we scan during brute force lookup. Common mistakes include not handling null neighbors, circular references, or incorrect undirectedness (needs mutual links). Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val=0, neighbors=None): self.val = val; self.neighbors = neighbors if neighbors is not None else []. This standalone concept can be practiced by creating small graphs.

examples.input: Create graph: Node1 connected to Node2
examples.trace: Step 1: node1 = Node(1); node2 = Node(2). Step 2: node1.neighbors.append(node2); node2.neighbors.append(node1). Result: node1.neighbors = [node2], node2.neighbors = [node1].

examples.input: Create graph: Node1 connected to Node2 and Node3
examples.trace: Step 1: node1=Node(1); node2=Node(2); node3=Node(3). Step 2: node1.neighbors.extend([node2, node3]); node2.neighbors.append(node1); node3.neighbors.append(node1). Result as above.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Node Creation & Linking
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of instantiating node objects and modifying their neighbor lists.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves creating new node instances and adding them to neighbor lists, which is foundational for building and manipulating graphs. The intuition is that in object-oriented programming, we define a class to represent nodes, then use constructors to create objects and list operations to establish connections. For cloning, we need to create new node objects with same values and set up their neighbor relationships. Step-by-step: 1. Define Node class with constructor. 2. To create a node: newNode = Node(val). 3. To add a neighbor: node.neighbors.append(otherNode). 4. To access neighbors: for nb in node.neighbors. Time: O(1) for creation and appending, O(k) for iterating k neighbors. Space: O(1) per node. Connection to L3: This operation is used to construct the graph representation. Common mistakes include not initializing neighbors list, adding same node multiple times, or creating cycles incorrectly. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.neighbors = []; node1 = Node(1); node2 = Node(2); node1.neighbors.append(node2). This standalone problem can be coded to practice object manipulation.

ladders.explanation: In this problem, a graph node is an object with a value and a list of references to other nodes (adjacency list). Cloning requires creating new objects while preserving the same connection structure. Understanding object identity vs value equality is key.

approaches.name: DFS with HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Use depth-first search to traverse the graph, maintaining a hash map to map original nodes to their clones, ensuring each node is cloned exactly once and neighbors are linked efficiently. This approach recursively clones nodes and their adjacency lists.

ladders.title: Full Problem — DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to clone an undirected graph given its adjacency list representation using DFS recursion and a hash map.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires creating a deep copy of an undirected graph where each node contains a value and a list of neighbors. The key challenge is avoiding infinite recursion when cloning cyclic graphs and ensuring each original node maps to exactly one clone. The DFS with HashMap approach solves this elegantly by using depth-first traversal to recursively clone nodes while maintaining a dictionary that maps original nodes to their clones. The intuition is that during DFS, when we encounter a node that's already been cloned (present in the hash map), we return its existing clone instead of creating a duplicate. This ensures each node is cloned exactly once regardless of how many times it's reached via different paths. The algorithm starts with the input node, creates its clone, adds the mapping to the hash map, then recursively clones all neighbors by calling the same function on each neighbor. For each neighbor, if it's already cloned, we simply retrieve the clone from the hash map; otherwise, we recursively clone it. Finally, we add the neighbor clones to the current node's neighbor list. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes plus edges, as we visit each node and edge exactly once. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack and hash map. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive cloning function that handles the entire graph. Common mistakes include not handling null input, forgetting to add the initial node to the hash map before recursing on neighbors, and incorrectly linking neighbors by adding original nodes instead of clones. Real code pattern: def cloneGraph(node): if not node: return None; visited = {}; def dfs(original): if original in visited: return visited[original]; clone = Node(original.val); visited[original] = clone; for neighbor in original.neighbors: clone.neighbors.append(dfs(neighbor)); return clone; return dfs(node).

examples.input: Graph with 3 nodes: Node1(val=1, neighbors=[Node2, Node3]), Node2(val=2, neighbors=[Node1, Node3]), Node3(val=3, neighbors=[Node1, Node2])
examples.output: Cloned graph with same structure but new Node objects
examples.trace:
1. Start with Node1. Create clone Node1', map Node1->Node1' in hash map. 2. Recurse on Node1's first neighbor Node2. Node2 not in map, create clone Node2', map Node2->Node2'. 3. Recurse on Node2's first neighbor Node1. Node1 in map, return Node1'. Add Node1' to Node2''s neighbors. 4. Recurse on Node2's second neighbor Node3. Node3 not in map, create clone Node3', map Node3->Node3'. 5. Recurse on Node3's first neighbor Node1. Node1 in map, return Node1'. Add Node1' to Node3''s neighbors. 6. Recurse on Node3's second neighbor Node2. Node2 in map, return Node2'. Add Node2' to Node3''s neighbors. 7. Back to Node2: Add Node3' to Node2''s neighbors
... (simplified)

examples.trace: 1. Start with Node1. Create clone Node1', map Node1->Node1' in hash map. 2. Node1 has no neighbors, so no recursion. 3. Return Node1' as final clone.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS/BFS Cloning
ladders.desc: The recursive function that clones a single node and all its neighbors using DFS with memoization.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the solution: a recursive function that takes an original node and returns its clone, ensuring each node is cloned exactly once through memoization. The intuition is that graph cloning is naturally recursive—to clone a node, we need to clone its neighbors, which in turn need their neighbors cloned. The hash map acts as memoization storage to prevent re-cloning nodes we've already processed. The algorithm: 1. Check if the node is in the visited hash map; if yes, return its clone immediately (base case). 2. Otherwise, create a new node with the same value. 3. Add the mapping from original to clone in the hash map (crucial to do this before recursing on neighbors to handle cycles). 4. For each neighbor in the original node's neighbors list, recursively call this function on the neighbor, and add the returned clone to the new node's neighbors list. 5. Return the new node. Time complexity for this function on a single call is O(k) where k is the number of neighbors, but overall across all calls it's O(n) as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion depth and hash map. This connects to L0 by being the core function called from the main solution. Common mistakes include adding the mapping after recursing (causing infinite recursion on cycles), not checking for null nodes, and incorrectly passing parameters. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, visited): if node in visited: return visited[node]; clone = Node(node.val); visited[node] = clone; for neighbor in node.neighbors: clone.neighbors.append(dfs(neighbor, visited)); return clone.

examples.trace: 1. Node1 not in visited. Create Node1', map Node1->Node1'. 2. For neighbor Node2: call dfs(Node2). 3. Node2 not in visited. Create Node2', map Node2->Node2'. 4. For neighbor Node1: call dfs(Node1). 5. Node1 in visited, return Node1'. Add Node1' to Node2''s neighbors. 6. Return Node2'. 7. Add Node2' to Node1''s neighbors. 8. Return Node1'.
1. Node1 in visited, return Node1' immediately.

testCases.input: Node1(val=1, neighbors=[Node2, Node3]), visited={Node2: Node2', Node3: Node3'}

testCases.expected: Node1' with two references to Node2'

testCases.input: Node1(val=1, neighbors=[Node2]), Node2(val=2, neighbors=[Node3]), Node3(val=3, neighbors=[]), visited={}
testCases.expected: Node1' with chain to Node2' and Node3'

ladders.title: Core Logic — Memoization / Mapping
ladders.desc: Using a hash map to store original-to-clone mappings, ensuring each node is cloned only once.
ladders.explanation: This is the central insight that makes the DFS approach work efficiently for cyclic graphs. The intuition is that in a graph, nodes can be reached via multiple paths (especially in cycles). Without memoization, DFS would repeatedly clone the same node, causing exponential time complexity and incorrect structures with duplicate nodes. The hash map solves this by acting as a cache: when we first clone a node, we store the mapping; when we encounter the same node again, we retrieve the existing clone instead of creating a new one. This ensures uniqueness and prevents infinite recursion. The algorithm involves: 1. Initialize an empty hash map. 2. When cloning a node, check if it's already in the map. 3. If not, create clone, store mapping, then process neighbors. 4. If yes, return the stored clone immediately. The key is storing the mapping BEFORE recursing on neighbors—this is critical for cycles, as a node might be reached via a neighbor before its own cloning is complete. Time complexity for lookup/insert is O(1) average, contributing to overall O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing n mappings. This connects to L1 as the mechanism used within the recursive function. Common mistakes include storing mapping after recursion (causing stack overflow on cycles), using wrong keys (like values instead of node objects), and not clearing the map between tests. Real code pattern: visited = {}; if node in visited: return visited[node]; clone = Node(node.val); visited[node] = clone; ...

examples.trace: 1. Start cloning Node1: not in map, create Node1', map Node1->Node1'. 2. Clone Node1's neighbor Node2: not in map, create Node2', map Node2->Node2'. 3. Clone Node2's neighbor Node1: Node1 in map, return Node1'. 4. Map now has two entries, preventing re-cloning.
1. Clone Node1: map Node1->Node1'. 2. Clone Node2: map Node2->Node2'. 3. Clone Node3: map Node3->Node3'. 4. Each node added once.

testCases.input: Map={Node1: Node1'}, node=Node1 with neighbor Node2
testCases.expected: Return Node1', map unchanged (neighbor not added yet)

testCases.input: Map={}, node=Node1, then same node again
Map={Node1: Node1'}, node=Node2 with neighbor Node1
testCases.expected: Add Node2->Node2', retrieve Node1' for neighbor

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Traversing all nodes in a graph using depth-first search recursion.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves visiting every node in a graph exactly once using DFS recursion, which is foundational for the cloning algorithm. The intuition is that graphs are recursive structures: from a node, we visit its neighbors, then recursively visit their neighbors, and so on. DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. For cloning, we need to traverse all nodes to copy them. The algorithm: 1. Start at a given node. 2. Mark it as visited (to avoid cycles). 3. For each neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS on that neighbor. In the cloning context, 'visiting' means cloning, and we use a hash map for visited tracking. Time complexity is O(V+E) where V is vertices and E is edges, as each node and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack and visited set. This connects to L2 as the traversal method that uses memoization. Common mistakes include not marking nodes as visited before recursing (causing infinite loops on cycles), missing edge cases like null graphs, and stack overflow on deep graphs. Real code pattern: def dfs(node, visited): if node in visited: return; visited.add(node); for neighbor in node.neighbors: dfs(neighbor, visited).

examples.trace: 1. Start at Node1, mark visited. 2. Recurse on Node2, mark visited. 3. Recurse on Node3, mark visited. 4. Backtrack to Node2, then Node1.
1. Start at Node1, mark visited. 2. Recurse on Node2, mark visited. 3. Recurse on Node1's neighbor Node1, already visited, return. 4. Done.

testCases.expected: Visited: Node1, Node2, Node3 (order may vary)
Start at Node1: visited Node1; Node2 not visited unless start there

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Node Creation & Linking
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of instantiating node objects and populating their neighbor lists.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves the mechanical steps of creating new node instances and setting up their adjacency relationships, which is essential for building the cloned graph. The intuition is that a graph is composed of nodes with values and references to neighbors; cloning requires creating new node objects with copied values and correctly linking them to represent the same structure. The algorithm: 1. Create a new node object with the same value as the original. 2. Initialize an empty neighbors list for the new node. 3. For each neighbor in the original node's neighbors list, add the corresponding clone to the new node's neighbors list. This must be done after all neighbors are cloned to ensure we add clone references, not originals. Time complexity is O(k) for a node with k neighbors. Space complexity is O(1) per node plus O(k) for the neighbor list. This connects to L3 as the action performed during DFS traversal. Common mistakes include shallow copying (adding original nodes to neighbor lists), not initializing the neighbor list, and modifying the original graph accidentally. Real code pattern: clone = Node(original.val); clone.neighbors = []; for neighbor in original.neighbors: clone.neighbors.append(neighbor_clone).

examples.trace: 1. Create Node1' with val=1. 2. Initialize empty neighbors list. 3. For neighbor Node2, add Node2' (assuming already cloned). 4. For neighbor Node3, add Node3'. 5. Result: Node1' with two neighbors.
1. Create Node1' with val=5. 2. Initialize empty neighbors list. 3. No neighbors to add. 4. Result: isolated node.

testCases.input: Node(val=1, neighbors=[Node2, Node3]), Node2(val=2, neighbors=[]), Node3(val=3, neighbors=[])

testCases.expected: Node' with two references to same neighbor
Node1' linked to Node2', Node2' linked to Node1'
Node' with null val, no neighbors

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Nodes & References
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graph nodes as objects with values and neighbor references.
ladders.explanation: In this problem, a graph node is an object with a value and a list of references to other nodes (adjacency list). Cloning requires creating new objects while preserving the same connection structure. Understanding object identity vs value equality is key.

examples.input: Simple graph: Node1(val=1, neighbors=[Node2]), Node2(val=2, neighbors=[])
examples.trace: Node1 has value 1 and one neighbor: Node2. Node2 has value 2 and no neighbors. This represents a directed edge from Node1 to Node2 (though in problem it's undirected, but representation is similar).
Node1's neighbors list contains a reference to Node1 itself, creating a cycle of length 1.

testCases.input: Node1(val=1, neighbors=[Node2, Node3]), Node2(val=2, neighbors=[]), Node3(val=3, neighbors=[])
testCases.expected: Node1 connected to two leaf nodes

testCases.input: Node1(val=1, neighbors=[Node2]), Node2(val=2, neighbors=[Node3]), Node3(val=3, neighbors=[])
Node1(val=10, neighbors=[Node2, Node3]), Node2(val=20, neighbors=[Node1]), Node3(val=30, neighbors=[Node1])
testCases.expected: Star shape with Node1 at center

testCases.input: Node1(val=1, neighbors=[Node2, Node3]), Node2(val=2, neighbors=[Node3]), Node3(val=3, neighbors=[Node1, Node2])

approaches.name: BFS with HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Use breadth-first search with a queue to traverse the graph level by level, employing a hash map to store mappings from original nodes to clones, cloning nodes and their neighbors iteratively. This method is iterative and avoids deep recursion stacks.

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS
ladders.desc: Complete iterative solution to clone a graph using BFS traversal and a hash map for node mapping.
ladders.explanation: This solution clones an undirected graph represented by adjacency lists using BFS with a hash map. The intuition is to traverse the graph level by level, creating clones of nodes as we encounter them and using a hash map to maintain a mapping from original nodes to their clones to avoid duplication and correctly set up neighbor relationships. We start with the given node, enqueue it, and create its clone, storing the mapping. Then, while the queue is not empty, we dequeue a node, iterate through its neighbors, and for each neighbor not yet cloned, we create its clone, store the mapping, and enqueue it. Finally, we add the clone of each neighbor to the clone's neighbor list. This ensures all nodes and edges are cloned exactly once. The time complexity is O(n + e) where n is the number of nodes and e is the number of edges, simplified to O(n) since e can be up to O(n^2) but in adjacency list representation, we process each edge once. Space complexity is O(n) for the queue and hash map. Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of BFS traversal with neighbor processing. Common mistakes include not handling null input, forgetting to mark nodes as visited via the hash map, or incorrectly linking neighbors. Real code pattern: Use a queue for BFS, a dictionary for mapping, and iterate through neighbors to clone and connect.

examples.trace:
Input: Node 1 with neighbors [2,4]. Step 1: Initialize queue = [1], map = {1: clone1}. Step 2: Dequeue 1, neighbors [2,4]. For neighbor 2: not in map, clone2 created, map = {1:clone1, 2:clone2}, queue = [2]. For neighbor 4: not in map, clone4 created, map = {1:clone1, 2:clone2, 4:clone4}, queue = [2,4]. Add clone2 and clone4 to clone1.neighbors. Step 3: Dequeue 2, neighbors [1,3]. For neighbor 1: in map, skip cloning. For neighbor 3: not in map, clone3 created, map updated, queue = [4,3]. Add clone1 and clone3 to clone2.neighbors. Step 4: Dequeue 4, neighbors [1,3]. Both in map, add clone1 and clone3 to clone4.neighbors. Step 5: Dequeue 3, ne
... (simplified)

Input: Single node 1 with no neighbors. Step 1: Initialize queue = [1], map = {1: clone1}. Step 2: Dequeue 1, neighbors []. No neighbors to process. Queue empty, return clone1 with empty neighbor list.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS/BFS Cloning
ladders.desc: Implement BFS to traverse a graph and process each node's neighbors for cloning.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the BFS traversal component of the full solution. The intuition is to systematically visit all nodes in the graph level by level using a queue, ensuring each node is processed exactly once. For each node dequeued, we iterate through its neighbors: if a neighbor hasn't been visited (i.e., not in the hash map), we create its clone, mark it as visited by adding to the map, and enqueue it for future processing. This avoids infinite loops in cyclic graphs and ensures all nodes are cloned. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize a queue with the start node and a hash map with the start node mapped to its clone. 2. While queue is not empty: a. Dequeue a node. b. For each neighbor: if not in map, create clone, add to map, enqueue neighbor. c. Add clone of each neighbor to the current node's clone neighbor list. Time complexity: O(n + e) for visiting all nodes and edges. Space complexity: O(n) for queue and map. Connection to L0: This is the core loop that drives the cloning process in the full problem. Common mistakes: Not using a queue for BFS, incorrectly checking visitation, or missing neighbor connections. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque for queue, dict for map, and for loops for neighbors.

examples.trace: Step 1: queue = [1], map = {1:clone1}. Step 2: dequeue 1, neighbors [2,3]. Neighbor 2 not in map: clone2 created, map={1:clone1,2:clone2}, queue=[2]. Neighbor 3 not in map: clone3 created, map updated, queue=[2,3]. Add clone2,clone3 to clone1.neighbors. Step 3: dequeue 2, neighbors [1]. Neighbor 1 in map, skip. Add clone1 to clone2.neighbors. Step 4: dequeue 3, neighbors [2]. Neighbor 2 in map, skip. Add clone2 to clone3.neighbors. Queue empty.

Step 1: queue=[1], map={1:clone1}. Step 2: dequeue 1, neighbors [2]. Neighbor 2 not in map: clone2 created, map updated, queue=[2]. Add clone2 to clone1.neighbors. Step 3: dequeue 2, neighbors [1]. Neighbor 1 in map, skip. Add clone1 to clone2.neighbors. Queue empty.

testCases.input: Start: 1, neighbors [2,3,4]; Graph: star with center 1
Start: 1, neighbors [2]; Graph: 1-2-3 (1 connected to 2, 2 to 3)
Start: 1, neighbors [2,2]; Graph: self-loop? (invalid, but test)
Start: 1, neighbors [2,3,4]; Graph: complete graph K4

ladders.title: Core Logic — Memoization / Mapping
ladders.desc: Use a hash map to track original nodes to their clones, preventing duplicates and enabling neighbor linking.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves using a hash map (dictionary) to maintain a one-to-one mapping from each original graph node to its cloned counterpart. The intuition is that during graph traversal, we need to ensure each node is cloned exactly once, even if encountered multiple times through different paths (e.g., in cycles). The hash map serves as a visited set and a lookup table: when we first encounter a node, we create its clone and store the mapping; on subsequent encounters, we skip cloning and use the existing clone from the map to set up neighbor relationships correctly. This avoids infinite recursion or duplication. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty hash map. 2. When processing a node, check if it's in the map; if not, create a clone and add the mapping. 3. When processing neighbors, use the map to get or create clones. Time complexity: O(1) average for map operations, contributing to overall O(n). Space complexity: O(n) for storing n mappings. Connection to L1: This mapping is essential within the BFS loop to manage node visitation and cloning. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check the map before cloning, leading to duplicate nodes, or not updating the map promptly. Real code pattern: Use a dict with node as key and clone as value, with checks like 'if node not in map: map[node] = Node(node.val)'.

examples.input: Original nodes: 1,2 with edge 1-2; Process node 1 then neighbor 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with node 1, map empty. Clone1 created, map={1:clone1}. Step 2: Process neighbor 2 of node 1, map does not have 2. Clone2 created, map={1:clone1, 2:clone2}. Step 3: When processing node 2 later, map has 2, so use clone2 directly.

Step 1: Node 1, map empty, clone1 created, map={1:clone1}. Step 2: Neighbor 2 of 1, map no 2, clone2 created, map updated. Step 3: Neighbor 3 of 2, map no 3, clone3 created, map updated. Step 4: Neighbor 1 of 3, map has 1, use clone1 without new clone.

testCases.input: Nodes: 1,2; Map initially empty, add 1 then 2
Nodes: 1,2; Add 1, then check if 2 in map
Nodes: 1,2,3; Add 1,2, then get clone for 3
testCases.expected: KeyError or None, since not in map

testCases.input: Nodes: 1,2,3; Map full, get clone for 2

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand and work with graphs represented as adjacency lists, where each node has a list of neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers the graph data structure used in the problem: an adjacency list representation. In this representation, the graph is defined as a list of nodes, where each node has a value and a list of references to its neighboring nodes. For example, in Python, a Node class might have attributes 'val' and 'neighbors'. The intuition is that this representation efficiently stores sparse graphs and allows easy traversal of neighbors. It's crucial for the cloning problem because we need to replicate both the nodes and the edges between them. Step-by-step: 1. Define a Node class with val and neighbors. 2. Construct a graph by creating nodes and setting their neighbor lists. 3. Traverse by accessing node.neighbors. Time complexity: O(1) to access a node's neighbors. Space complexity: O(n + e) to store nodes and edges. Connection to L2: The hash map maps original Node objects to cloned Node objects in this representation. Common mistakes: Confusing adjacency lists with adjacency matrices, or not handling null or empty neighbor lists. Real code pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val=0, neighbors=None): self.val = val; self.neighbors = neighbors if neighbors is not None else [].

examples.input: Create a graph with two nodes: 1 and 2 connected.
examples.trace: Step 1: Instantiate Node1 with val=1, neighbors=[]. Step 2: Instantiate Node2 with val=2, neighbors=[]. Step 3: Set Node1.neighbors = [Node2]. Step 4: Set Node2.neighbors = [Node1]. Now Node1 and Node2 reference each other.

examples.input: Create a single node with no neighbors.
examples.trace: Step 1: Instantiate Node1 with val=1, neighbors=None (defaults to []). Node1.neighbors is an empty list.

testCases.input: Node values: 1,2,3,4; Connect 1 to all others

testCases.expected: Both have each other as neighbor

testCases.input: Node values: 1,2,3; Connect all to all (complete graph)
Node values: 1,2; Set neighbors to empty list initially
testCases.expected: Both have empty neighbors, then can add later

testCases.input: Node values: 1,2,3; Connect 1 to 2, but 2 not connected back
testCases.expected: Node1.neighbors=[Node2], Node2.neighbors=[] (directed, but problem is undirected)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Node Creation & Linking
ladders.desc: Perform enqueue and dequeue operations using a queue to manage BFS traversal order.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a queue (First-In-First-Out data structure) to control the order of node processing in BFS. The intuition is that BFS explores nodes level by level: starting from a source node, we visit all its neighbors before moving to their neighbors. A queue ensures this by enqueuing nodes as we discover them and dequeuing to process them in the order they were added. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty queue. 2. Enqueue the start node. 3. While queue is not empty: a. Dequeue a node to process it. b. Enqueue its unvisited neighbors. This guarantees each node is processed exactly once if combined with a visited set. Time complexity: O(1) per enqueue/dequeue. Space complexity: O(n) for queue storage. Connection to L3: In graph traversal, we use the queue to manage Node objects from the adjacency list. Common mistakes: Using a list as a queue with pop(0) (inefficient), or not checking queue emptiness. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque for efficient O(1) operations: queue = deque(); queue.append(node); node = queue.popleft().

examples.input: Start node: A, neighbors: B,C; Process BFS
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue = [A]. Step 2: Dequeue A, process neighbors B and C: enqueue B, then C. Queue = [B, C]. Step 3: Dequeue B, process its neighbors (none new), queue = [C]. Step 4: Dequeue C, process, queue empty.
Step 1: queue = [1]. Step 2: dequeue 1, enqueue neighbor 2, queue = [2]. Step 3: dequeue 2, queue empty.

testCases.input: Start empty, enqueue X, dequeue X
Enqueue A,B,C; dequeue once; enqueue D; dequeue all

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Nodes & References
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graph nodes as objects with values and neighbor references.
ladders.explanation: In this problem, a graph node is an object with a value and a list of references to other nodes (adjacency list). Cloning requires creating new objects while preserving the same connection structure. Understanding object identity vs value equality is key.

examples.input: Create two nodes with values 1 and 2, and link 1 to 2.
examples.trace: Step 1: Define Node class. Step 2: Instantiate node1 = Node(1). Step 3: Instantiate node2 = Node(2). Step 4: Set node1.neighbors = [node2]. Now node1 references node2, but node2 does not reference node1 unless set.

examples.input: Create a node with value 3 and no links.
examples.trace: Step 1: Define Node class. Step 2: Instantiate node3 = Node(3). By default, neighbors is an empty list. Node3 exists independently.

testCases.input: Create Node with val=10, set neighbors to empty list
Create two Nodes: A(val=1), B(val=2); Link A to B
Create Node C(val=3), don't set neighbors
Create Node D(val=4), set neighbors to [Node E(val=5)]
Create Node F(val=6), link to itself
Create Nodes G(val=7), H(val=8); Link mutually
